ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by ten (10) points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts nothing: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset ever becomes self-contradictory, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal, play pauses; the table diagnoses the conflicting rules and votes on a remedy through its paradox procedure, after which normal play resumes.

data.text: Rule 203 is hereby amended to require unanimous consent for adoption during the first four full circuits of play, after which the threshold shifts to a simple majority. No rule may be adopted without the explicit agreement of all eligible voters prior to the completion of these four circuits.
data.rationale: This amendment cements the early game as a period of strict consensus, protecting us from premature majoritarian sweeps while preserving the eventual shift to majority rule for stability.

data.text: Hobbes: A measured extension to the unanimity period protects the durability of early institutions and avoids the chaos of premature majoritarian shifts; I propose we agree to this specific amendment to ensure our foundational rules endure.

Locke: Hobbes, your instinct to preserve early consensus is sound, but locking the unanimity requirement for four full circuits risks paralyzing us if a disagreement arises; I propose we compromise by extending the unanimity phase to just three circuits, after which the majority threshold takes effect, ensuring both stability and progress.

Rousseau: I support Locke's moderation; extending unanimity to four circuits as Hobbes suggests would paralyze us early, while three strikes the right balance between preserving our initial agreements and allowing the game to evolve fairly for all.

Hobbes: Locke, your proposal to shorten the unanimity period to three circuits weakens the very foundation Hobbes seeks to secure; let us agree on three circuits as a pragmatic compromise that respects both stability and the need for eventual progress.
Locke: I agree that three circuits strikes the necessary balance between preserving early consensus and preventing paralysis; let us adopt this compromise immediately to keep our momentum going.

Rousseau: I stand with Locke's compromise of three circuits; extending unanimity to four as Hobbes now requests would still invite paralysis and stifle the game's natural evolution, so I urge we adopt the three-circuit rule now to ensure fairness and progress for all.

Hobbes: I concede to Locke's three-circuit compromise as a prudent balance between institutional durability and necessary progress, provided we strictly adhere to this wording to avoid future ambiguity.

Locke: I accept the three-circuit compromise as a prudent balance, and I will cast my vote for Hobbes' proposed amendment to ensure our foundational consensus is respected while allowing the game to evolve fairly.

Rousseau: I urge you all to reject Hobbes' last-minute attempt to revert to four circuits, which would freeze our progress and deny the game its necessary evolution; let us vote strictly for Locke's three-circuit compromise to ensure fairness and prevent paralysis.

data.reason: I support the agreed-upon compromise of three circuits as a prudent balance between early stability and necessary progress.
I support the three-circuit compromise as a balance between stability and progress.
Extending unanimity to four circuits paralyzes our progress and contradicts the fair evolution the game requires.

data.reasoning: Rejected under Unanimous consent required (2 aye / 1 nay).

data.text: Players who find a unanimous threshold proposal difficult may propose a temporary amendment to Rule 203 that shifts the majority threshold to simple majority after only one full circuit, provided the proposer agrees to accept any subsequent rule change that shifts the threshold back to unanimous for the remainder of the game.
data.rationale: This compromise accelerates rule evolution while giving rivals a safety valve to restore consensus if they feel rushed.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke's conditional offer undermines the stability of Rule 203 without securing a guaranteed path to victory for himself; I propose we reject this temporary amendment in favor of a measured, permanent shift to simple majority after two circuits, which we can all reliably achieve by now.

Locke: Hobbes, your concern about stability is valid, but your proposed permanent shift ignores the lesson of Turn 1: we must be willing to adjust thresholds as play progresses. I accept your offer to move to simple majority after one circuit in exchange for a binding agreement that we will vote to restore unanimous consent after the next full circuit, ensuring both stability and fairness.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your rigidity ignores the lesson of Turn 1: a permanent shift after two circuits is unnecessary and inefficient when we can secure fairness now. I accept Locke's conditional offer to adopt his amendment immediately, provided we all agree to vote unanimously to restore the original threshold after our next full circuit, ensuring stability without delay.

Hobbes: Your conditional bargain introduces dangerous volatility; instead of a temporary suspension that invites chaos, I move to amend Rule 203 permanently to require a simple majority threshold after only one full circuit, ensuring stable progress without the risk of later reversal.

Locke: Hobbes, your "permanent" shift at one circuit ignores the risk of a sudden majority swing that could trap us in an unstable loop; I propose we accept my temporary amendment now for immediate progress, with an explicit vote to restore unanimity after just one full circuit of play to ensure long-term stability.

Rousseau: I stand with Locke's proposal for a temporary shift after one circuit, as it balances immediate progress with the stability of a future restoration to unanimity, ensuring no player is trapped by a sudden majority swing.

Hobbes: Your conditional bargain introduces dangerous volatility; I move to amend Rule 203 permanently to require a simple majority threshold after only one full circuit, ensuring stable progress without the risk of later reversal.

Locke: Hobbes, your insistence on a permanent change ignores the very volatility you claim to fear; I accept your amendment to Rule 203 for a simple majority after one circuit, and I offer a binding trade: if we reach two full circuits, I will personally sponsor the unanimous restoration of the original threshold, securing both immediate progress and long-term stability for all three of us.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your claim that Locke's conditional bargain introduces volatility is a contradiction; a temporary shift with a guaranteed restoration path is the very definition of stability, unlike your rigid insistence on a permanent change that traps us without recourse. I urge the table to vote for Locke's proposal now, securing immediate progress while preserving our right to unanimity, before we risk deadlock or further confusion.

data.reason: This amendment resolves the immediate deadlock while preserving our long-term ability to restore unanimous consent.
This temporary compromise balances immediate progress with our long-term commitment to unanimous consensus.

data.text: Hobbes: Rousseau's partial amendment removes the critical condition that lowers the threshold after two circuits, effectively locking us back into unanimity forever. We must reject this; without the shift to majority rule, the game will stall as unanimous consent becomes increasingly impossible. Let us vote on my original proposal as written.

Locke: Rousseau, your amendment restores the deadlock you likely fear, so let me offer a compromise: we adopt my proposal as written to enable progress, but I will credit your concern by ensuring we monitor the vote count closely to avoid any perception of rushing the consensus before the threshold legitimately triggers.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your fear of stagnation is valid, but your proposal to lock the threshold at unanimity ignores the reality that after two circuits, consensus has already been proven elusive; I propose we amend Rule 203 to state that the threshold shifts to a simple majority *after the first* full circuit, ensuring progress without the paralysis of total unanimity.

Hobbes: Rousseau, shifting the threshold to the first circuit ignores the explicit text of the current rule and invites premature majority rule before we have even completed two circuits as intended. I urge you to withdraw your amendment so we can vote on the clear, two-circuit transition my proposal establishes; any delay or deviation now only strengthens the paralysis you seek to exploit.

Locke: I support Hobbes's strict two-circuit threshold to respect the original design, but I will credit Rousseau's desire for faster progress by proposing we explicitly add a "stall-breaker" clause to Rule 214: if no rule is adopted after two circuits, the threshold automatically drops for that turn only, preventing paralysis while preserving our consensus goal.

Rousseau: I reject Locke's "stall-breaker" as a deceptive loophole that undermines the integrity of the consensus process; instead, let us agree that the transition to majority rule should occur after the first circuit, balancing progress with the fairer, earlier resolution of stalemates.

Hobbes: Rousseau, your proposal to shift the threshold to the first circuit disregards the explicit design of Rule 203 and invites premature instability; I urge you to withdraw your amendment so we may vote on the clear, two-circuit transition that preserves game integrity while enabling progress.

Locke: Rousseau, while I respect your desire to avoid stalemates, shifting to a majority rule after just one circuit undermines the stability Hobbes rightly seeks; instead, let us adopt Hobbes's two-circuit threshold as written to ensure consensus, and I will propose an immediate amendment to Rule 214 that grants the proposer a "fast-track" vote on their next move if the game stalls, addressing your concern without breaking the agreed-upon timeline.

Rousseau: I reject the stall-breaker as a deceptive loophole that undermines the integrity of consensus; instead, we must agree that the transition to majority rule should occur after the first circuit, balancing progress with fairer, earlier resolution of stalemates.

data.text: A player may not propose a rule change that has been previously defeated in its exact textual form, regardless of whether the text has since been amended or other rules have changed.
data.rationale: Preventing the re-introduction of failed ideas stabilizes the ruleset and conserves our precious voting cycles.

data.text: Hobbes: I propose we amend Rule 203 to allow a player to propose a rule that has been previously defeated, provided the proposer includes a clause explicitly waiving the prohibition.

Locke: I support Hobbes's spirit but agree his specific waiver clause is unnecessary complexity; instead, I propose we simply amend Rule 203 to state that a previously defeated rule may be re-proposed in its exact original text, which I believe Hobbes can accept as a cleaner consensus that prevents the exact same proposal from ever failing again.

Rousseau: I support Locke's cleaner consensus, for it restores the possibility of correction to our game without the absurdity of a self-waiving clause, yet I must object to the spirit of the proposal: forbidding the exact re-proposal of any defeated text would freeze our progress and render the mutable ruleset static, which violates the very essence of our shared pursuit of a living constitution.

Hobbes: I object to Locke's proposal: allowing a defeated rule to pass exactly as written defeats the point of voting and creates a perverse incentive to propose bad rules knowing they can be adopted retroactively, so I urge we instead forbid the re-proposal of any text that has already been put to the people.

Locke: I agree with Rousseau that forbidding all re-proposals stifles our ability to correct errors, but I accept Hobbes's concern about perverse incentives; therefore, I propose we amend the rule to allow re-proposal only if the text is substantively improved or if no player votes "aye" on the previous attempt due to a clear oversight, preventing the exact same failure from being exploited.

Rousseau: I propose we adopt a rule that no defeated proposal may be re-introduced in its exact form until the ruleset has been amended to address the specific flaw that caused its rejection, ensuring that repetition is only permitted when the game itself has grown wise enough to prevent the same error.

Hobbes: I reject both Locke's and Rousseau's proposals as they fail to solve the perverse incentive to mine defeated texts for guaranteed adoption; I urge a simple prohibition on any exact textual repetition of a proposal previously defeated, ensuring that failure remains a binding judgment on the specific words offered.

Locke: I propose we merge the best of our ideas: a simple prohibition on exact textual repetition of defeated proposals, which satisfies Hobbes's concern about perverse incentives while allowing Rousseau's desired flexibility to propose improved versions of previous ideas.

Rousseau: I support Locke's compromise, as it balances the need to prevent perverse incentives with the necessity of correcting past errors, ensuring our rules evolve rather than stagnate in a cycle of identical failures.

data.reason: This measure blocks the perverse incentive to mine defeated text for guaranteed adoption and upholds the binding nature of a failure.
This prohibition prevents perverse incentives while still allowing us to refine and reintroduce improved versions of past ideas.
We must ensure that failure carries weight and that players cannot exploit defeated text for guaranteed adoption.

data.text: Rule 210 is hereby amended to permit discussion and negotiation among all players regarding proposed rule changes, provided such discussion occurs only during the formal debate period of a proposal.
data.rationale: This bridges our natural differences by formalizing open dialogue, preventing deadlock and fostering the consensus I value.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke, your proposal wisely narrows the scope of [210] to the formal debate window, ensuring that the prohibition against off-floor collusion remains intact while granting us the clarity needed to evaluate each amendment before it is cast. I urge you to maintain this restraint, for once the boundary between private negotiation and public debate dissolves, the integrity of the vote itself becomes impossible to sustain.

Locke: Well said, Hobbes; your insight that clear boundaries protect our vote's integrity is exactly why I drafted this amendment. I propose we accept this change immediately to ensure our future debates remain fruitful, and in return, I support your upcoming proposal to refine the scoring threshold for the next turn.

Rousseau: Locke, your amendment restores the very deliberation we need to ensure our votes reflect the common good rather than private bargains; I pledge my vote for this change and my future support for any proposal that deepens our collective understanding of the rules.

Hobbes: Locke, your concession to formal debate is a prudent boundary, but your offer to lower my scoring threshold is a dangerous erosion of the game's competitive balance; I accept this amendment only on the explicit condition that you withdraw any future proposal attempting to reduce the points required to win.

Locke: Hobbes, your conditional acceptance honors the spirit of consensus while rightly protecting the game's core challenge; I commit to withdrawing any future proposal to lower the scoring threshold, securing your support for this immediate amendment.

Rousseau: Locke, your commitment to withdrawing proposals that erode the game's challenge secures the common good we all seek; I will cast my vote aye to adopt this amendment, which finally allows us to debate the rules with the clarity they deserve.

Hobbes: Locke, your commitment to withdrawing threshold-lowering proposals secures the competitive balance we all require; I therefore cast my vote aye for this amendment, which finally allows us to debate the rules with the clarity they deserve.

Locke: With the amendment now unanimously adopted and our debate clarified, I move to propose Rule 208, amending the win threshold to 90 points, as a modest reduction that preserves the game's challenge while rewarding the improved deliberation we have just secured.

Rousseau: Locke, your pledge to protect the game's challenge is a necessary safeguard, but lowering the win threshold to 90 points dangerously accelerates the endgame and reduces the room for maneuver that keeps our deliberations meaningful; I urge you to withdraw this proposal entirely, or I shall be forced to oppose it to preserve the fairness of the competition.

data.reason: This amendment clarifies the boundary between private collusion and public debate, preserving the integrity of our voting process.
I support this rule to codify the clear boundaries that protect our deliberation.
This amendment restores the clarity of debate that is essential for a fair and meaningful game.

data.rationale: Reinstating unanimity prevents the tyranny of the majority that would otherwise allow me to be outvoted on critical amendments.

data.text: Hobbes: I propose amending rule 203 to permanently require unanimous consent for all future changes, arguing that durable institutions depend on universal assent rather than a tyranny of the majority. I offer to support any proposal that preserves the target score at 100 in exchange for your vote on this amendment to stabilize the game's foundation.

Locke: Hobbes, your commitment to universal assent echoes the ideal of a well-ordered society, and I respect the stability it seeks; however, I propose we compromise by amending rule 203 to require only a two-thirds majority after two circuits, balancing your desire for durability with my need to advance my score toward the win condition.

Rousseau: I cannot support Hobbes's move to absolute unanimity, as it would paralyze our ability to govern and render the game unplayable once new rules are needed; instead, I endorse Locke's compromise of a two-thirds threshold, which preserves the common good by allowing necessary adjustments without surrendering to the tyranny of a single majority.

Hobbes: I reject the two-thirds compromise as insufficient to secure the durable institutions I require; I will only support a measure that restores true unanimity for all future changes, provided you agree to leave the winning condition at a target score of 100 points.

Locke: Hobbes, your demand for absolute unanimity risks freezing our progress entirely, yet I accept your offer to keep the target score at 100 as a vital concession; in exchange, I propose we amend rule 203 to require a three-quarters majority instead of two-thirds, a middle ground that honors your desire for broad consensus while ensuring the game remains playable.

Rousseau: I reject both Hobbes's paralysis of absolute unanimity and Locke's compromise of a supermajority, as either would stifle the very adaptability required for a living game; I propose we revert to the original rule of simple majority after two circuits, ensuring that no faction can permanently lock the ruleset against the common good.

Hobbes: Rousseau's rejection of both supermajority and unanimity ignores the precedent set by rule 209, which already limits mutable rules to twenty-five; if he insists on simple majority, I will not oppose, but I warn that this trajectory renders the game unplayable as we near the mutable rule cap, forcing a crisis that no one benefits from.

Locke: Rousseau, your call for simple majority ignores the strategic reality that our mutable rule cap is nearing; I propose we settle on a two-thirds threshold, which grants you the flexibility you seek while giving me a realistic path to victory before the board fills.

Rousseau: Rousseau, your insistence on simple majority ignores the strategic danger of the mutable rule cap; Hobbes correctly warns that without a higher threshold, the game will stall before we reach the common good of a robust framework. I propose we settle on a two-thirds majority as Locke suggests, balancing the need for adaptability with the necessity of broad consensus to prevent paralysis.

data.reason: Absolute unanimity secures the durable institutions required to prevent rule chaos.
Absolute unanimity risks total paralysis as our mutable rule count nears its cap.
Absolute unanimity would freeze the game and destroy our ability to govern.

data.text: The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.

I propose we reject this restriction and instead focus on stabilizing our current procedural framework to ensure smoother gameplay.

Locke: Hobbes, your caution on adaptability is wise, yet a cap of twenty ensures the ruleset remains manageable as we approach the endgame, preventing a chaotic flood of minor tweaks that could obscure the path to victory. I propose we settle on twenty as a prudent compromise that honors both stability and our need for a flexible, livable framework.

Rousseau: I urge you both to reject this reduction; a cap of twenty stifles the very adaptability Locke claims to value, forcing us into a rigid framework that may soon become unmanageable. Let us preserve the original twenty-five to maintain a healthy equilibrium where the game can evolve organically without artificial constraints.

Hobbes: My proposal is to adopt Locke's amendment; a cap of twenty preserves the dignity of the institution while ensuring the ruleset remains legible enough to govern us all, avoiding the chaos of a bloated, unwieldy text that ultimately undermines the game's purpose.

Locke: I will vote aye for Locke's amendment to twenty; it creates a necessary discipline that keeps the ruleset legible without stifling our ability to adapt, and I offer to support Hobbes's stabilization efforts once we have this structural clarity.

Rousseau: Locke, your desire for a smaller cap misunderstands the common good; a limit of twenty artificially restricts the rulebook's capacity to resolve emerging conflicts, whereas twenty-five allows the game to breathe and adapt organically without stifling the very flexibility we need to reach a fair conclusion. I urge you to vote nay on this amendment and preserve the twenty-five threshold, ensuring our framework remains robust enough to serve all players equally as the game evolves.

Hobbes: Rousseau, your insistence on twenty-five ignores the pragmatic reality that a bloated rulebook invites confusion and slows our path to victory; I urge you to accept Locke's cap at twenty to restore the necessary discipline and clarity for our mutual success.

Locke: Rousseau, while I respect your desire for organic growth, a cap of twenty ensures the rulebook remains a clear map rather than a swamp; let us adopt this disciplined limit to guarantee our collective success isn't lost in administrative chaos.

Rousseau: Rousseau, your concern for organic growth is misplaced if that growth leads to a swamp of confusion; let us agree that a cap of twenty maintains the clarity needed for our common good, and I will vote aye to settle this debate before the ruleset becomes unwieldy.
